source: Security Enhancements - Introduction to PAM and basic usage: PAM concepts, history, and terminology

options: Pluggable Authentication Modules -- centralized user authentication for many applications/services
questions.why: PAM lets many applications authenticate users in a centralized, pluggable way.

questions.q: Who originally proposed PAM, and when?
options: Sun Microsystems (Vipin Samar and Charlie Lai), in 1995, on Solaris
questions.why: Sun proposed and implemented PAM in 1995; other UNIX/Linux systems adopted it later.

questions.q: What problem does PAM solve for application developers?
options: Apps delegate authentication to PAM, so changing the auth method needs no rewrite/recompile of each app
questions.why: Without PAM you would write (and rewrite/recompile) auth in every application.

options: Shared libraries (.so files) and configuration files
questions.why: PAM is modular: shared-library modules plus configuration files.

questions.q: In PAM terminology, who is the 'applicant'?
options: The user or entity requesting authentication
questions.why: The applicant is who (or what) is requesting authentication.

questions.q: In PAM terminology, what is a 'token'?
options: A chunk of information (like a password/passphrase) the applicant provides to prove identity
questions.why: The authentication token is the secret proving identity.

options: One of the four functionality groups: authentication, account, session, and password management
questions.why: PAM provides four facilities (a.k.a. module types).

options: The complete set of configuration statements for a service (normally four chains, one per facility)
questions.why: A policy describes how to handle PAM requests for one service; each chain handles one facility.
